title: Laminated cinnamon morning buns
subtitle: Laminated croissant dough rolled with cinnamon sugar
excerpt: Cinnamon morning buns built on laminated croissant dough rolled with cinnamon sugar, sliced and proofed in a muffin tin, and finished with a hot sugar glaze. Twelve buns from a two-day schedule.
sourceNotes: The morning bun is a Bay Area bakery form (popularised by Tartine in the early 2000s) that uses laminated croissant dough rolled with cinnamon sugar. No single pre-1928 public-domain source documents the exact form. The recipe synthesises the established laminated-dough method from Escoffier and the cinnamon-sugar-and-roll technique from Hannah Glasse (1747).

recipe.freezeNotes: Shaped, unproofed buns freeze well; defrost overnight in the fridge and proof from cold.

content.text: A morning bun is laminated croissant dough rolled with cinnamon sugar instead of plain. The technique starts from the croissant recipe (which see for the lamination steps); on shaping day, the rested dough is rolled out, sprinkled with cinnamon sugar, rolled into a sausage, cut into 12 spirals, and pressed into a muffin tin to proof and bake.
The muffin tin forces the spiral to puff upward into a tall layered bun. A hot sugar glaze brushed over the buns straight from the oven gives the characteristic sticky shell.

Follow the croissant dough method: mix the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, cold {{whole-milk}} and first {{unsalted-butter}} in a stand mixer for 6 minutes. Shape, wrap, chill 1 hour. Beat the second {{unsalted-butter}} into a 20×15 cm rectangle and chill. Lock the butter into the dough; do three single folds with 30-minute rests between each. Chill overnight.
Day 2: roll with cinnamon sugar, shape

Mix the {{soft-brown-sugar}} with the first 18 g of {{cinnamon-ground}}. Roll the laminated dough to a 45×30 cm rectangle, 4 mm thick. Brush lightly with water. Scatter the cinnamon sugar evenly over. From a long edge, roll up tightly into a sausage. Slice into 12 spirals. Press each cut-side-up into a well-greased muffin tin hole. Proof at room temperature for 2 hours until puffed and the spirals are pressing against the sides of the tin.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Bake for 25–30 minutes; the buns should be deep gold all over and the cinnamon-sugar layers should be visible. Mix the second {{caster-sugar}} with the second {{cinnamon-ground}} in a shallow bowl. While the buns are still hot, lift each from the tin and toss in the cinnamon-sugar to coat. Cool on a rack.
